title: Bona Fide Checks: Verifying Customers and Suppliers
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: How UK and EU quality teams run GDP bona fide checks to verify customers and suppliers, with a step-by-step method, red flags and QMS controls.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, lifted by a single subtle green accent. Soft natural light rakes across a polished laboratory bench where a gloved hand holds a sealed, unmarked medicine carton beside a slightly out-of-focus tablet screen suggesting an authorisation register being cross-checked. Shallow depth of field draws the eye to the carton and the verifying hand; cool reflective surfaces, faint condensation and clean, precise shadows convey scrutiny and trust. Composition is 4:3, elegant and uncluttered, with no text, no logos and no readable characters anywhere in the frame.

faqs.q: What is a bona fide check in pharmaceutical distribution?
faqs.a: It is a documented verification that a customer or supplier is a genuine, identifiable legal entity and holds the correct authorisation for the medicines being traded. Under Good Distribution Practice you may only buy from and supply to appropriately licensed parties, and you must be able to evidence that you confirmed this at source. It is a core GDP control and a frontline defence against falsified or diverted product entering the legitimate supply chain.

faqs.q: How are customer checks different from supplier checks?
faqs.a: Supplier checks confirm that the party selling to you is authorised to do so and that the goods are genuine, while customer checks confirm that the recipient is actually entitled to receive medicinal products. A frequent failing is treating customer due diligence as a sales formality rather than applying the same rigour used to qualify suppliers. Both directions must be verified against the regulator's own registers, not on documents supplied by the counterparty alone.

faqs.q: How often should bona fide checks be repeated?
faqs.a: Verification should follow a risk-based cycle rather than a single check at onboarding, because authorisations can be varied, suspended or revoked at any time. Defined triggers, such as a change of ownership, a new product category, a lapse in trading or any regulatory action, should prompt re-verification. Higher-risk traders and third-country relationships warrant tighter intervals, and the schedule should be set out in your quality management system.
